_how_to_add: Copy the format from journal-template.md and add new entries under entries[]

entries.title: Replacing Strong with something I actually own
body: I have been using the Strong app to track lifting for a while. It works, but I do not own the data in any meaningful way, and I wanted the ability to close the loop between logging a workout and getting recommendations for the next one.

Today I shipped that. LiftCycle is a Next.js app with a Supabase backend. You log your sets, and overnight an agent I have running analyzes the last two sessions per lift and applies linear progression rules. Open the app next session and your weights are already filled in.

The progression logic is simple on purpose: if you hit all reps cleanly two sessions in a row, add weight. If you missed reps, hold. If you have stalled three sessions at the same weight, flag it. No percentages, no periodization charts, just a system that keeps moving forward until it cannot.

The part I am most happy about is that I own all of it. The app is mine, the database is mine, the progression script runs on my own infrastructure. No subscription, no vendor lock-in, no dark patterns nudging me toward a premium tier.

I also started a paper trading bot for BTC/USD today. DCA plus momentum signals using a 20-period SMA and RSI. One hundred dollars of virtual capital, running hourly. Checking back in a week to see if the strategy is worth putting real money behind.
Two different projects, same underlying idea: automate the boring part so you can focus on the part that actually requires you.

entries.title: Why this journal exists
body: I want this space to feel more like a workbench than a portfolio.
This is where I capture what I am building, what I am learning, and where I get stuck.
The goal is consistency, not polish.

entries.title: forHumanity reached operational baseline
body: Shipped CI, hardening, observability, backup/recovery, and launch checklists.
The biggest shift: this stopped being just code and became an operable system.
Next question is user adoption and real-world feedback loops.

entries.title: What I want to explore next
body: I am interested in building practical AI operators that help run projects end to end.
Not demos. Systems that can deploy, monitor, and recover.
This journal is where those experiments will be tracked.

entries.title: AI, uncertainty, and what it means to stay human
body: I often think about the impact AI will have on life, society, work, everything, and truth.
It feels unavoidable. Everywhere you look, there is an article about the positives and the negatives.
Just this past weekend, there was a piece in the NY Times about an 85-year-old woman whose quality of life improved after getting a companion robot.

At the same time, there was an opinion piece mourning the loss of human connection and pointing out that even the people building large language systems do not really know what will happen or where this all goes.
I cannot help feeling like this is how evolution works. There are no certainties. No guarantees.
We cannot know everything, and we cannot control everything yet. Still, we try, and that tension is fascinating.
As humans, we want to control as much as possible while innovating as fast as possible in ways that are inherently uncontrollable.
I understand the fear and uncertainty.
I do find it concerning when people choose a chatbot or avatar over real human connection.
To me, that reflects a deeper societal failure, that people have become so isolated that those outlets are the only way they feel heard and seen.
At the same time, I believe AI can help us advance and explore what it really means to be human, and what humanity is, beyond productivity gains, faster output, or corporate enrichment.
The real question is: How do we evolve as humanity while developing and bringing this potential new life form into existence?
That is the question I look forward to thinking about as we move forward.
